questions.question: Explain the terms 'seek time' and 'rotational latency' in the context of magnetic disk performance. How do they contribute to total disk access time?
keyPoints: Seek time is time taken by disk arm to move head to correct track
Rotational latency is waiting time for desired sector to rotate under head
Total access time = Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time
Seek time typically 3-15ms, rotational latency depends on RPM

questions.question: Describe the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm with an example showing how it services requests.
keyPoints: Also known as elevator algorithm
Moves from one end to other servicing all requests in path
Then reverses direction
Reduces average seek time compared to FCFS
Example with track positions would demonstrate the head movement

questions.question: What is wear leveling in SSDs? Why is it necessary?
keyPoints: Wear leveling distributes writes evenly across flash cells
Prevents premature wear of specific blocks
NAND flash has limited P/E cycles (1000-100000)
Extends SSD lifespan and maintains performance
Uses logical to physical block mapping

questions.question: Differentiate between magnetic hard disks and Solid State Drives in terms of data storage mechanism.
keyPoints: Magnetic disks use rotating platters and moving read/write heads
SSDs use NAND flash memory cells storing data as charge
Magnetic disks have mechanical parts; SSDs are entirely electronic
SSDs have no seek time or rotational latency
SSDs cannot overwrite in place; require erase before write

questions.question: What is the TRIM command? How does it help maintain SSD performance?
keyPoints: TRIM is an ATA command allowing OS to inform SSD of deleted data
SSD can then mark those blocks as free without waiting for new write
Allows SSD controller to proactively clean invalid pages
Without TRIM, SSD performance degrades over time
Essential for maintaining consistent performance

questions.question: Explain RAID 5 with reference to fault tolerance and performance.
keyPoints: Uses striping with distributed parity
Requires minimum 3 disks
Parity distributed across all drives
Can tolerate failure of one disk
Read performance good; write slower due to parity calculation
Suitable for applications needing balance of performance and redundancy

questions.question: What is garbage collection in SSDs? Explain why it is needed.
keyPoints: Process of reclaiming space from invalid pages
Flash memory cannot overwrite - must erase entire block first
Valid data in block must be moved before erasing
Background process performed by SSD controller
Essential for maintaining write performance
Can cause write amplification

questions.question: Explain the following disk scheduling algorithms with examples: FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN. Compare them in terms of performance and fairness.
keyPoints: FCFS: Services requests in arrival order, simple but poor performance
SSTF: Chooses nearest request, minimum seek time but starvation possible
SCAN: Like elevator, reverses at ends, good average performance
C-SCAN: Circular, equal wait times, uniform service
Performance comparison with examples
Fairness: FCFS fair but slow, SSTF unfair to distant requests, SCAN and C-SCAN more uniform
Real-world considerations: workload patterns

questions.question: Describe the internal architecture of an SSD including flash memory organization (blocks, pages), controller functions, and the operations of wear leveling and garbage collection.
keyPoints: Flash memory structure: dies → planes → blocks → pages
Pages typically 2KB-16KB, blocks contain 64-256 pages
Cannot write to already written page without erase
Controller handles: mapping, wear leveling, garbage collection, ECC
Wear leveling maps logical addresses to physical locations evenly
Garbage collection moves valid data, erases blocks
Write amplification concept and impact
TRIM command role

questions.question: Discuss RAID technology covering RAID levels 0, 1, 5, and 6. Include details on striping, mirroring, parity, fault tolerance, and performance characteristics.
keyPoints: RAID 0: Striping only, no redundancy, improves performance, no fault tolerance
RAID 1: Mirroring, exact copy on second disk, good read, slower write
RAID 5: Striping with distributed parity, minimum 3 disks, good read, slower write due to parity
RAID 6: Double parity, can tolerate 2 disk failures, minimum 4 disks
Comparison table of characteristics
Trade-offs between performance, capacity, reliability
Common use cases for each level

questions.question: Calculate the total disk access time for reading a file consisting of 10,000 sectors (each sector = 512 bytes) from a disk with the following specifications: Seek time = 8ms, Rotational speed = 7200 RPM, Transfer rate = 50 MB/s. Show all calculations.
keyPoints: Calculate rotational latency: Average = 1/2 × (60/7200) = 4.17ms
Total seek time = 8ms (assuming single seek for all sectors in same track)
For consecutive sectors on same track, only one seek and one rotational latency needed
Total = Seek + Latency + Transfer = 8 + 4.17 + 102.4 = 114.57ms
Note: If sectors span tracks, additional seek times would be needed
